key_concerns: Higher rate of early deaths in the ide-cel arm, particularly within the first 9 months (18% vs 11% in ITT)
Many early deaths occurred before ide-cel infusion, raising concerns about patient selection and manufacturing delays
No statistically significant OS benefit, with crossing Kaplan-Meier curves indicating potential early detriment
The Applicant's post-hoc OS analyses rely on unverifiable assumptions and are not convincing
Uncertainty remains whether PFS benefit outweighs the risk of early mortality
key_positives: Statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in PFS (HR 0.49, median 13.3 vs 4.4 months)
High overall response rate (71% vs 42%) with durable responses
Consistent PFS benefit across subgroups and by independent review
Later OS data (median 41.4 vs 37.9 months) do not show a significant survival disadvantage

evidence_notes: KarMMa-3 met its primary endpoint, demonstrating a statistically significant improvement in PFS
The rate of deaths in the first 9 months post randomization was higher in the ide-cel arm (45/254; 18%) compared to the SOC arm (15/132; 11%)
Visual inspection of the Kaplan Meier plot for OS indicates that ... there were more early deaths occurring in the ide-cel arm compared to the SOC arm
The Applicant's analyses do not provide convincing evidence that ide-cel treatment provides OS benefit
FDA considers the risks associated with administration of the treatment to be integral to the benefit-risk assessment
A higher proportion of subjects died before disease progression in the ide-cel arm (8%) compared to the standard of care arm (3%)

The Applicant's post-hoc OS analyses rely on unverifiable assumptions and are not convincing

questions_framing.question: Is the risk-benefit assessment for idecabtagene vicleucel favorable?

document.title: March 15, 2024 Meeting of the Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee- FDA Briefing Document (ABECMA)

signal.label: NEGATIVE
rationale: Concerns: Higher rate of early deaths in the ide-cel arm, particularly within the first 9 months (18% vs 11% in ITT); Many early deaths occurred before ide-cel infusion, raising concerns about patient selection and manufacturing delays; No statistically significant OS benefit, with crossing Kaplan-Meier curves indicating potential early detriment; The Applicant's post-hoc OS analyses rely on unverifiable assumptions and are not convincing; Uncertainty remains whether PFS benefit outweighs the risk of early mortality

Positives: Statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in PFS (HR 0.49, median 13.3 vs 4.4 months); High overall response rate (71% vs 42%) with durable responses; Consistent PFS benefit across subgroups and by independent review; Later OS data (median 41.4 vs 37.9 months) do not show a significant survival disadvantage
